sentences.text: So where was I?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yes.
Between-- around 4:00 was a good time to begin it.
So we-- when we planned the revolt, we took into consideration the very well-known discipline of the Nazi officers, of the German officers.
And you see, in a way, in the brashness, they were so assured of themselves.
How could a miserable Jew fight and beat SS, you know?

So in-- and because there was some-- such abundance of craftsmen-- they had little shops, a tailor shop, shoemaker shop, which worked for the Nazis, besides killing people, they had the little shops, and they always had something on order.
So we planned that the tailor shop should order, should ask, make  an appointment with the head for that time.
The shoemaker shop, for another Nazi, in that time.
And they made appointments.
And we were very, very, very well sure that he will come at the time.
If a German will tell you, he will come at that time, you could be 99% he would come.
Besides this, the Germans actually picked up nicer stuff and sent it to Germany.
So you find-- so if he finds something in there, while sorting some nice stuff, the Germans picked it up.
And when he sent a package to Germany, to his family, he included it.
So we were aware that the Germans are looking mostly for leather stuff and so on.
So we decided to use this, too, this trick to lure them to places to be killed.
I was a young kid.
I shouldn't be involved in the organization, direct.
But I was involved for two reasons.
Because I was the boss of this-- they called me fireman of this place, where I burned the papers.
And this was a place where a German could be easily called in and killed.
Nobody will see it.
And Leon Feldhendler, although he was from Zolkiewka, but he lived in Izbica, and was a friend of my dad, so he did know me very well.
And you see, the younger kids were able-- there was a few of us young people-- we were able to move around more easier than the grownup people.
So I did know about that.
He told me.
Leon called  me, and he told me in that we are doing this and this.
I remember, on the 13th of October, because there are windows, and somebody could look in inside, push the papers, everything, push against the windows, and nobody could look in.
Because they want to kill a German over there.
We would call him under pretense that something is wrong with the ovens, something is cracking, and so on.
When he will come in, we can-- well, of course, otherwise, I wouldn't let anybody in there.
So he told me, Kali Mali will come with-- Kali Mali is a name of a Russian prisoner of war who was in the organization.
He will come.
And he will hide behind the oven.
And when the Nazi comes in, you show him, so he should turn his back to the Jews, hidden Jews.
And he will be killed with an axe.
And I agreed.
And so I was aware of what's happened.
And I was very much scared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember the plan was, actually, that after we assembled for the rollcall, which would take place 5:30, we should wait till 5:30, because some Germans could survive.
And at that point, they will notice that another friend is missing.
We should actually make the escape 15 minutes earlier.
And we should escape in an orderly manner.
It was planned that we should assemble in normal walking groups and march to the main gate, because Sobibor, when you go in, had little like cages and cages.
There was cage, surrounded by barbed-wires, where we used to sleep.
In another cage, we used to walk.
And gates lead from one cage to another one.
So the plan was that we march out, like, like, it would be a German order.
And we'd march to the main gate.
And they were hoping that the guards will think it's a German order, because we go so brazenly straight.
And then we would come close to the gate, where the machine gun is.
And they will see that something is not OK, the guards.
It will be too late for them.
And we will overwhelm them.
It didn't happen, just because we were discovered.
How it happened, I will tell you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] October 13, I remember, about 3 o'clock, I heard some laughing, shooting.
And I was afraid that we were discovered.

But what's happened-- a group of Nazis that come from the nearby camp of Osada just to have a good drink, you know, because they had a  lot of liquor in Sobibor, but because there were more Germans, now, the escape was called off for the 13th and was set for the next day.
The next day, I was scared that we will be betrayed, again, in some way, because we were once.
I didn't tell you the story, but we were once betrayed.
By Jews.
It's a long story.
He did later become the oberkapo, the traitor.
And we killed him later.
We poisoned him.
It's a story, but, at I said, if I ever need to tell everything, it would take days.
So I'll tell you the most important.
For the next day-- the next day, I was impatient.
I was scared.
I was not scared, really, to die, because I had time to get used to it.
I was scared that I will suffer.
We, prisoners, we always said, we will not go to the gas chamber.
We would rather want to be shot at.
So I was aware but shot, you would be wounded.
So I prayed for a  fast death.
And I was scared.
And I remember, I was-- it was close to 4 o'clock.
I was sitting, looking through the window of my place, when I see Kali Mali, the Russian officer, and another.
And he was accompanied by a kapo, Bunio.
He was  in the organization.
And I thought they were going to my place, the way it was planned.
But they didn't.
The went straight to the sorting place, the clothing sorting place.
And later, Sender, a Jew from Lodz, did come to my place and told me, Tom, we changed the plans, because your place is very close to the barbed-wires.
And the guards could notice the German coming in but  not coming out.
So we decided it's too dangerous to do it here.

But he directed me to a gate, which lead from the sorting area to another part of the camp, to stay there and not to let people go out, because he was afraid somebody will notice something and could go out or whatever-- betray.
So I left my place.
I went  to the gate.
They were standing there, watching.
There was a Dutch fellow standing close by.
And we talked.
And suddenly, I see Wolf, a Nazi, while, actually, I was going to my place, to the gate.
I'd seen already a friend of mine was standing in front of the SS men and told them that he should-- he find a nice, leather coat.
He should go try it  on.
And he went.
When I went to the gate, the German was already getting into the storeroom to try the leather coat on.
And, of course, he was killed.
I wasn't inside, but, a few minutes later, I'd seen the people coming out with bloody shoes and  so on.
So he was killed in there.
But if I remember from the film, he really did try on the coat.
He did.
[INAUDIBLE] That, I want to tell you.
But from later, from the people with which I escaped, which have seen it, they told me that he did go in.
And he did put the hands  in the sleeves of the coat.
And the moment he put it in, his hands were tied-up, like in a straitjacket.
And he was killed  with an axe.
And he was thrown-- a lot of clothing was thrown over him.
As a matter of fact, the Nazis, when later, they looked for their dead comrades, they were unable to find him.
And he was listed not as killed.
He was listed as lost in the first report to Krakow.
Anyway, and later, everything, what's happened, I wasn't anymore scared.
I know there's no way back, because in the beginning, I thought, maybe you shouldn't do it?
[INAUDIBLE] And I know, no matter what, it has happened.
And I went out to fetch another Nazi.
And there was a Nazi who delivered food.
He was the driver of a little railroad, a small railroad, which connected two parts of the camp.
And I called him to try a leather coat.
And he left.
And the same thing happened to him.
And I remember, I went to this-- to this little railroad, like the mines have such-- coal mines have this little railroad.
So I looked inside, and there were foodstuffs, canned foodstuff, canned foodstuff, fish and so on, sardines.
So I put  it in my pocket.
And I remember, I had a friend, his name was [POLISH] Karolek, Blind Karl.
And Blind Karl looks at me and said, you're packing like for a picnic.
You're sure you will survive.
So I told him, if I survive, I will use it.
If not, I don't know.
How heavy is it?
We used it, later, though, and so on.
Anyway, in the matter of one hour, [?
Cohen ?] had come from another part of the camp, where the-- where the craftsmen were working, and told us that the deputy commandant was killed.
Anyway, by 5 o'clock, most of them were killed, of the Nazis.
No, they didn't know.
It was quiet.
At 5 o'clock, as usual, a prisoner went to the tower and put a signal out, with a trumpet, to end the work.
The kapos assembled it, like every day, because the rule was, it should look like every day.
And the two kapos, which were  in the organization, were ordered even to beat us.
That's every day, if somebody is slow.
And we assembled in groups, and we marched to the kitchen, to the main yard.
It was 5 o'clock.
And now, I remember, when I marched in, I was shocked to see how many people don't know what's happened.
When the [INAUDIBLE] in that part of the camp, we were 500 at that time.
If there were 500 people, I guarantee about 450 stand in the line for food, not knowing that practically 95% of the Germans  are killed.
But now, I'm told.
One told another one.
And simple-- and they're kissing each other.
And I remember, an older man-- I say, older.
Maybe he was.
His daughter was 17.
Maybe he was 50 years old.
He was a tailor.
And he was, I remember, walking back and forth and holding his hand and breaking his fingers, you know.
Why do we need it  for?
We could live another two weeks, another three weeks.
Why do  we need  it for?
You know, when you're close to die, every second is precious.
You don't imagine.
You had never the feeling.
You will never understand it, even.
And so, I remember this.
I remember people-- talking about heroism.
Told you about the people picking up the-- [INAUDIBLE] the money pieces.
I remember people going back to the barracks, putting-- picking up the prayer shawls, which they hide, under the threat of death, taking them out, putting on the head, and praying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They decide not to escape, because they realized theirs no salvation outside, either.
Their families were killed.
They didn't commit suicide, probably, because the Jewish religion forbids-- forbids suicides.
But they didn't want to live.
But they put on the prayer shawls and started to pray, the last minute, facing the  Germans.
And probably, when the Germans surrounded them, they were praying.
They were defying them.
That we are still Jews and believe  in our God.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I think, something like this.
[INAUDIBLE]
So there was a lot of heroism going on.
And you can't portray this, that some didn't want to escape.
So-- and soon, Sasha stepped on a table, had a speech.
And  I was standing about five feet from him.
And again, I was begging God, let me survive.
And he said the last words.
Remember, they said in the film, this is what I was-- what I did remember.
I told them.
And he said, whoever will survive, his duty is to tell the world what's happened here.
And I promised him, God, if you will let me survive, I will never relax telling.
And suddenly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we heard a shot from far away.
I didn't see this.
But later, I find out that a German discovered a dead body of his comrade.
And he sounded the alarm.
But it was too late for them, anyway.
But--
How many Germans?
About 12 from Sobibor.
Anyway, so we realized something happened.
So the plan, was a little bit-- didn't work at that point, because the plan was to march out.
So we realized there was something wrong.
At that point, he stopped, and he stepped on the table and had the speech, Sasha.
And suddenly, somebody start to yell, hurrah!
First, a single voice, somebody probably wasn't able to stand it, anymore, the tension.
Because Sasha was still speaking when he said, hurrah.
And everybody picked it up, hurrah, hurrah.
And they run to the gate.
I did run to the gate.
And I remember a Nazi did come driving in with a bike.
He was immediately thrown from the bike, killed, the weapon taken away.
And I was running with the main group to the main-- to the main gate.
And I remember Frenzel shooting at us with a machine gun.
I remember another Nazi, with his back against the canteen-- we had a canteen-- was shooting with a pistol while he was backing up.
And I see, here, a friend fall, here, there.
I hear the whistle of the bullets.
So I backed up and start to run back.
And at that point, I don't remember what's happened.
But  the next thing I remember, I did find myself in a corridor.
You see, there were three barbed-wires running around Sobibor and a mine field.
The first two barbed-wires were fairly close.
But between the second and third, the last barbed-wire, there was a hallway, a guard for the Ukrainians with the dogs, for the guards.
By that  time, Ukrainians did escape, being afraid.
So I find myself-- probably, I went into the gate, where the Ukrainian went in.
I find myself in this hallway.
And I realize, luckily, I have two barbed-wires behind me, barbed-wire fences, only one front fence.
But in  the hand, I had only a knife.
I remember it was the inscription, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Probably--
Kosher for Pesach
--probably it was a rabbi's knife, because I find it  in the luggage.
And-- and what could I do with the knife?
I stood in front of the fence, barbed-wire fence.
Sasha was nearby, the leader, with a pistol.
He couldn't do much either.
And there was Szlomo with a  rifle.
I remember him shooting.
And no more people did come.
And finally, a guy did come with an axe and started to cut a hole in the fences.
And people started to go through the hole.
But as more and  more people did come in, the guards-- the guards which were from the towers, which we weren't able to reach, started to shoot.
And people weren't waiting in the line, weren't waiting.
They started to climb the fence.
And while it was-- at that time I thought, this is worst thing that could happen, that I am stuck.
I will be  in Sobibor, because the moment I went through this hole, at that point, the fence, under the heaviness of the bodies, of the people climbing, collapsed and pinned me down.
Now, I realized that this was my luck.
Because, while lying under the fence, people stepping over me, I've seen bodies taken apart by the mines.
And later, when everybody went through, I did try to climb out and wasn't able, because the barbs had embedded in my coat.
And luckily, I had a leather coat, because I picked it up from the storerooms.
And  it didn't go too deep.
But I just slid out from this leather coat and left the coat on the barbed-wires.
And I was running.
And I was probably the last one.
I  did fall down a couple times, I remember, in the field.
And I thought I'm hit .
But I did get up and run until I reached the forest.
I reached the forest.
I was running.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how long I did run.
But at that point, I did come to a group of people, and I stopped.
And this was our assembling point.
Sasha was with us.
And he counted us.
And we were about 50 people.
And he instructed us to not to smoke cigarettes.
And I remember, I've seen Kali Mali later.
And we met another group of prisoners, later.
We were afraid that this was a German setup, because, from far away, we've seen a group of people coming.
And we laid down.
We were afraid of the Germans.
But we find out that this was another group.
And we get together, so there was about 80 people together.
Now, a tragedy involved, which is not nice to talk about that.
But we can't talk only about nice things.
If my story should be a document, I must tell you everything.
Very bad things happened.
We did run for about three days.
In the daytime, we were sitting quietly, because planes were circling over the forest.
And  in two days, and it was when we were running, Sasha instructed us, we should run.
We should go one after another one, because he left-- before, we were running in groups.
This place was devastated.
And so, at one point, I remember a night, and we were going out, from the forest, through a meadow, to another forest.
And I turned my head, and I have seen this snake of people.
And I understood, no, it's impossible.
We will be caught.
But anyway, after a while, there was a meadow.
We relaxed there.
And Sasha said, now, we will go and find out where we are.
We heard a train passing by, not far away.
And he did asked one of us to collect money, because we had money.
Only the ones which is the banned organization picked up money from the storeroom.
And we did give him money.
He said, this is for food.
And he picked up 10 people, all the guns, all the people who had  the guns-- 10 people.
And we didn't let him.
We had a feeling, something.
We said, one person with a gun must stay.
Give us Szlomo Szmajzner from Pulawy.
And he didn't want to.
Even that, he wanted to take away the gun from him.
But he didn't give the gun.
And they left, promising that they'd find out, buy food, and come back.
They did never come back.
And finally, it starts to be daytime, daylight.
And we realized, we are out from the forest, left alone.
And a  terrible thing happened.
At that point, terrible things happened, because the healthy people, young people, did get together and tried to sneak away from the women, from the old people.
And we, in turn, didn't want to.
And we did try to go with the young people.
There were fights.
Finally, we were-- I did go immediately to my friend, Weizen, which was a friend in Sobibor.
And I told  him, listen, there's no way we could survive now.
We don't have weapons.
Do you want to go with me?
Yes.
Well, did they-- do you think that they had to continue their loyalty to each other?

Now, from the distance of the history, I know that, only, that not far from our place in the Parczew forest, so-called 'Parchev' forest, there were-- there was-- not far from Sobibor-- there was a group, Chil Grynszpan's group.
They were Jewish partisan groups with family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I imagine that he, with his experience in having-- we had rifles and guns, 10 of them, 11.
He could form a fighting unit, a very good fighting units, because we weren't simple people.
We were-- we have seen our end.
We  weren't dreaming.
We have seen it with our eyes.
We would fight till the last gasp.
He could go with us to the forest, be a leader.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he didn't do it.
When I met him in Russia, we talked about this, because, you see, I don't blame him.
He's still-- we are-- he was [INAUDIBLE] the one who saved us.
In the eyes of my eyes and the eyes of other survivors, he's a hero.
But still, there's a blemish.
First, he did leave us without-- he did go and leave us without guns.
He took money.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
He left.
When I talked with him, he said that his duty as a soldier was to return to the front-line, which is OK.
But why?
He's a person.
Whatever he said is reprinted, immediately, as true.
He said, in his book, that while we were in the meadow, they realized, there are too many people.
And  we can't survive.
So we divided in small groups, and everybody went his  way.
It's not true.
It was the way I told you.
Nevertheless, he has done a lot.
We can't demand, even from a hero, to be a superman.
He did feel bad when I talk about this, when I had an interview with him and talked about this.
So now, we are left.
While, I did go with Schmul, his friend-- [NON-ENGLISH] had attached himself-- and Kostman, and, later, little Dreszer.
We were eight people together.
We were sleeping in the bushes the fourth day, and suddenly-- and there was Mendel.
He was the baker  in Sobibor, a bigshot, a baker in Sobibor.
And suddenly, somebody pushed me with their arm.
Wake up.
Wake up.
Schwartz was the electrician in Sobibor.
He's going away.
Wake up.
And now I see that Schwartz and Karolek are trying to escape from us.
So all, we did get up.
We were strong people, you know?
And we run after him.
No.
So we had, with them, a deal.
You take Mendel.
Mendel, who was the baker and very strong in Sobibor and so on, he was, here, like a baby.
He always cried.
He was a very well-built man.
But I don't  know, he was crying and hysteric.
And he was no asset.
He was something which would give us-- so we told him, listen, Schwartz.
You take Mendel, and we will take Dreszer.
Dreszer is practically a child.
He was the youngest one in Sobibor.
So you will be three.
And we will be four, OK?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they didn't agree.
And after a while, we were watching them, and they did agree to the division.
I don't want to drag it on.
Anyway, they took Mendel.
And later, they break his legs, farther away.
And we heard his screams.
And people told me, survivors, that they were passing by, and Mendel was laying with broken legs.
Probably they broke his legs, so he shouldn't run after them.
I don't know.
But I assume it could be.
You don't know what  it mean to fight for life.
It's terrible.
No.
Yeah.
No, I was--
I wasn't a leader.
I was a kid, too.
And there were different groups, which picked up, get together, and some older people did go over to them.
We-- the two of us, Mendel and Dreszer did come.
So [INAUDIBLE] Dreszer is with  us now.
I'm sure they did.
I'm sure.
Because he kept running after them, and he was holding them back.
They took him.
And later, he did come back, crying, that they chased him away.
So we did go back to them and said, no, either all, or you take him.
So they finally took him and break his legs.
OK, now, we are four-- me, Schmul Weizen, Freddie Kostman, and Drezser.
Four, we go.
We were afraid to go to villages, because they could deliver you to the Nazis.
But finally, we see a little hut.
Very-- you don't see it now in Europe.
Maybe, in eastern Poland or Russia, maybe, you will see it.
But under one roof, store-roof, the cow and  the chicken and people are living.
Over there, we went in.
It was single-- and there's a woman.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], she says, God bless you, a standard greeting for the Catholics in Poland.
Could you sell us some food?
She's poor, but what she have, she will give us.
And she did give us.
And she refused to take money.
I'm a Christian.
I don't take money.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She refused to take money, not for food.
So [INAUDIBLE] I remember, there was a bed.
I've seen a cat.
And I was jealous of the cat.
She doesn't need to go out in the cold.
And even the Germans will come in, they probably will pet the cat.
But they will shoot me.
We left her.
I did want to give her a ring.
She refused.
And where should we go?
We are not far from Izbica.
We'll go to Izbica and see.
So, we were walking and walking and walking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see, sometimes stupidity worked better then some special plans.
We walked.
We were walking in the middle of the road in the night time.
Later, I find out-- as a  matter of fact, last year-- they discovered documents, Nazi documents, reports from the police, the Krakow, where they caught the Jews, and where they were finding the Jews.
So they put, the Nazis put [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soldiers on the corner roads and close to the forest, yeah, there.
But they didn't think that the  Jews would be stupid, going-- they're stupid, simple.
And we were right, nobody-- I mean, we didn't plan this, because but is so happened, that they didn't watch the main roads, only the crossroads.
Anyway, we made it close to Izbica.
We had a few problems on the way, which I want to skip, because telling about everything will take time.
We made it  close to Izbica.
We are hiding in a ditch.
And I told them, I'm going to Izbica to a Polish friend, ask him to hide us.
And they told me-- they took away all the money from me.
They told me to leave all the money, so I shouldn't hide by myself.
Anyway, this-- this is the way it was.
I left everything with them.
And I went to Izbica.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went into Izbica.
It was evening.
It was evening.
I didn't recognize the town, because, already, many houses were taken apart by Poles looking for gold.
I went to this lady, knocked on the door.
She opens.
She see me, immediately crossed herself.
God, what do you want?
I said, listen, you are a friend of our family.
Could you hide us?
No, She refused to hide us.
And I went back.
OK.
